origin: I got into programming in high school after taking a web design elective and realizing I was more interested in how things worked than how they looked. I taught myself JavaScript over a summer, built a few hobby projects, and decided to study CS at UT Austin. I worked part-time at Starbucks through my first two years to stay independent while I figured out what direction I wanted to go.
why_frontend: I landed on frontend engineering because it sits at the intersection of technical complexity and direct user impact — decisions about component architecture, accessibility, and performance are immediately visible to real people using the product. My internship at Meridian cemented that. Discovering that 34 components in a production product failed basic accessibility standards, and then systematically fixing them, made the work feel concrete and consequential in a way that pure backend work hadn't for me yet.
career_direction: I am most interested in frontend and UX engineering roles at product companies where the UI is genuinely complex — design systems, accessibility infrastructure, or component architecture work. I am less drawn to agencies or greenfield projects where the front end is mostly templating. Longer term I am curious about web performance engineering and developer tooling.
differentiators: I take accessibility seriously as an engineering discipline, not a compliance checkbox. Most engineers at my level have never run a proper WCAG audit or implemented ARIA patterns beyond the basics — I have, in a production environment under a real deadline. I also have design collaboration experience from working directly with Figma files and design tokens, which makes me more effective working alongside product designers than a typical engineer.
what_i_value_in_work: Design quality, engineering rigor, and a team that cares about both. I get frustrated when accessibility and performance are treated as afterthoughts or when the design-to-engineering handoff is chaotic. I want to work somewhere where the front end is treated as a first-class technical discipline.
problem_solving: I start with the user path — what is the actual interaction, what state changes are involved, what can go wrong. For accessibility problems specifically I use a screen reader before writing any code so I understand the experience I am trying to fix rather than guessing. For component architecture decisions I bias toward composability over cleverness — a flexible primitive that composes well is almost always better than a feature-rich monolithic component.
weaknesses: I have strong opinions about UI quality and can be slow to ship when I think something is not polished enough. I have had to calibrate when good enough actually is good enough, especially under time pressure. I also have a tendency to over-document my own design decisions in code reviews — I am working on trusting teammates to ask questions rather than pre-answering every possible concern.
what_energizes_me: The moment a component API clicks and you know it is going to be easy for other engineers to use correctly. Building the keyboard navigation for the modal component in my library and getting it right across all the edge cases — focus trap, escape key, scroll lock, return focus on close — was satisfying in a way that felt disproportionate to how small the feature was. The common thread is building things that work correctly for everyone, not just the happy path.
key_narrative_origin: The WCAG audit at Meridian. I went in expecting to find a handful of contrast issues and found 34 distinct violations affecting users with visual, motor, and cognitive disabilities. Fixing them systematically over 10 weeks — prioritizing by severity and user impact rather than ease — is where I learned to think about accessibility as infrastructure work rather than feature work.
key_narrative_conflict: During the campus events project, two teammates wanted to use a UI component library to move faster. I thought we should build core components from scratch because the library we were considering had known accessibility gaps and we were building for a university context where assistive technology use is higher than average. I put together a short comparison showing the specific gaps and the estimated time to patch them versus building the components ourselves. We built them ourselves. It cost us two extra days upfront and saved us a week of accessibility remediation at the end.
